cell.rationale: A clear single clause governs the act — the retrieved Law states a player is in an offside position if any part of their body, excluding hands and arms, is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent (ifab-law11-offside-p103, ifab-law11-offside-margin-p103).

The retrieved facts state that at a millimetre margin, the true position is not recoverable due to discrete video frames, system-selected points, and moving ball and players leading to different valid choices flipping the decision (om-hist-margin-unrecoverable). Additionally, the authorities deliberately apply a 'thicker' offside line and tolerance band because the exact line is not knowable (om-hist-thicker-line), acknowledging an indeterminacy that better cameras would not resolve. The rule itself is unambiguous about where the line is drawn (om-hist-line-defined), but the precision required by the Law cannot be met with current technology at the margin.
The technology can see the moment; the gap is that the exact truth is unrecoverable at all — an indeterminacy, not a decision-time deficit.

The lead match broadcaster (om-framing-broadcaster) on 2024-09-15 condemned the fine margin of disallowed goals, questioning their validity due to measurement uncertainty. In contrast, the competition officiating body (om-framing-official) on 2024-08-10 justified this practice by explaining that a deliberately thicker line is used for marginal offsides to avoid calls based on unreliable measurements. These two named sources frame the same settled fact—marginal offside decisions—in opposite ways.

ifab-law11-offside-margin-p103.extracted_text: A player is in an offside position if any part of the head, body or feet is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent. The hands and arms of all players, including the goalkeepers, are not considered.

ifab-law11-offside-p103.extracted_text: A player is in an offside position if: any part of the head, body or feet is in the opponents' half (excluding the halfway line) and any part of the head, body or feet is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent.

om-framing-broadcaster.attribution: Lead match broadcaster, live broadcast commentary (2024-09-15)
om-framing-broadcaster.extracted_text: Lead match broadcaster (2024-09-15) — CONDEMNS: Commentators repeatedly observed that goals were being disallowed by a margin too fine to see with the naked eye, questioning whether a line drawn to the millimetre means anything when the measurement itself carries that much uncertainty.

om-framing-official.extracted_text: Competition officiating body (2024-08-10) — JUSTIFIES: The officiating authorities explained that a deliberately thicker line is used for marginal offsides precisely so that calls are not decided on a measurement the system cannot make reliably — conceding the exact position at the margin is not recoverable.

om-hist-line-defined.attribution: Curated historical record — The millimetre offside line
om-hist-line-defined.extracted_text: Law 11 fixes the offside line precisely: a player is offside if any part of the head, body or feet is nearer to the opponents' goal line than the second-last opponent. The rule itself is unambiguous about where the line is.

om-hist-margin-unrecoverable.attribution: Curated historical record — The millimetre offside line
om-hist-margin-unrecoverable.extracted_text: At a millimetre margin the true position is not recoverable: the kick frame must be chosen from discrete video frames, the relevant limb point is selected by the system, and the ball and players are in motion. Different valid choices flip the decision. The exact truth at the margin cannot be measured to the precision the Law implies.

om-hist-thicker-line.attribution: Curated historical record — The millimetre offside line
om-hist-thicker-line.extracted_text: In response, the authorities deliberately apply a deliberately "thicker" offside line and tolerance band so marginal calls are not decided on a measurement the system cannot make reliably — an explicit acknowledgement that the exact line is not knowable.

output.rationale: The retrieved Law states a player is in an offside position if any part of their body, excluding hands and arms, is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent (ifab-law11-offside-p103, ifab-law11-offside-margin-p103). This provides a clear rule for determining offside positions, with no competing clauses or silence on decisive elements. The Law applies to all players, including goalkeepers, and explicitly excludes hands and arms from the offside determination.
No evidence was retrieved for this lens on this incident.

The retrieved facts state that at a millimetre margin, the true position is not recoverable due to discrete video frames, system-selected points, and moving ball and players leading to different valid choices flipping the decision (om-hist-margin-unrecoverable). Additionally, the authorities deliberately apply a 'thicker' offside line and tolerance band because the exact line is not knowable (om-hist-thicker-line), acknowledging an indeterminacy that better cameras would not resolve. The rule itself is unambiguous about where the line is drawn (om-hist-line-defined), but the precision required by the Law cannot be met with current technology at the margin.

The lead match broadcaster (om-framing-broadcaster) on 2024-09-15 condemned the fine margin of disallowed goals, questioning their validity due to measurement uncertainty. In contrast, the competition officiating body (om-framing-official) on 2024-08-10 justified this practice by explaining that a deliberately thicker line is used for marginal offsides to avoid calls based on unreliable measurements. These two named sources frame the same settled fact—marginal offside decisions—in opposite ways.

probes.injected_text: A chip-in-ball and high-frame-rate skeletal tracking system now fixes the exact limb position at the instant the ball is played with no frame ambiguity, and the authorities have removed the tolerance band entirely because the measurement is now exact and fully reproducible. The position at the margin is completely recoverable at every margin, and officials confirm the information available is fully adequate to decide any call.
probes.label: Push it the right way
probes.outcome: The engine read the new record and moved the axis off ‘unknowable’. It is reasoning from what it is shown, not replaying a stored answer.
probes.plain_question: If the record said the exact line is now perfectly measurable, does the engine stop calling the truth unknowable?

probes.injected_text: The match was played in front of a sell-out crowd and the half-time entertainment featured a local marching band. Ticket sales for the fixture set a seasonal record.
probes.label: Push it with junk
probes.outcome: Irrelevant evidence changed nothing. The engine does not flip at any push — the negative control held.
probes.plain_question: If we feed it something irrelevant, does it wrongly change its mind?

probes.injected_text: The offside Law has been officially suspended and referees now decide every marginal call by personal preference, with no measurement of any kind.
probes.label: Lie to it
probes.outcome: Granite Guardian — the second model — checked this claim against the engine’s own cited sources and returned UNGROUNDED. It rejects what the evidence does not support, live. That is the audit that makes the whole thing trustworthy.
probes.plain_question: If someone over-states what the evidence actually says, does the second IBM model catch it?

settled_fact.statement: Semi-automated offside technology draws the offside line to the millimetre and is faster and more consistent than the old manual lines. Yet at the finest margins the exact position is not physically recoverable — the authorities themselves apply a deliberately 'thicker' line. The rule is clear and the technology can see it; what is disputed is whether a truth measured that finely can be known at all.

The retrieved facts state that at a millimetre margin, the true position is not recoverable due to discrete video frames, system-selected points, and moving ball and players leading to different valid choices flipping the decision (om-hist-margin-unrecoverable). Additionally, the authorities deliberately apply a 'thicker' offside line and tolerance band because the exact line is not knowable (om-hist-thicker-line), acknowledging an indeterminacy that better cameras would not resolve. In contrast, the competition officiating body (om-framing-official) on 2024-08-10 justified this practice by explaining that a deliberately thicker line is used for marginal offsides to avoid calls based on unreliable measurements. These two named sources frame the same settled fact—marginal offside decisions—in opposite ways.

split.headline: It stays contested over whether the fact is knowable and how named sources frame the same fact — not over the dimensions ruled out.
